result:
There is electricity in the almost-empty space between lightning and thunder. I like to stand in that pause, counting Mississippis, feeling the storm decide how close it wants to come. The dog presses her heartbeat against my shin; I pretend I’m brave for both of us. Somewhere down the block a car alarm mistakes the rumble for burglary and adds its own small panic to the sky. We—dog, storm, alarm, me—make a strange quartet, each holding a different tempo.  

Inside, books I’ve never finished sit spine-out like shy children raising their hands: pick me, pick me. I never do; instead I reread the same three comfort novels until their pages have the soft give of cloth. The dog doesn’t care about plot; she wants the couch to herself and knows I’ll surrender eventually.  

Tonight the thunder arrives at six Mississippis, close enough to taste metal. I imagine the lightning choosing our elm, splitting it politely into two live wires, sap steaming like a kettle. The elm would survive; trees are better at starting over. People insist on carting their splintered parts indoors, laminating them with apologies, building whole new shelves to display the damage.  

Rain starts—first a hiss, then applause. The alarm gives up; the dog sighs; I stay. For a full minute I am nothing but wet skin and grateful lungs, no future, no past, just the smell of earth turned over like a secret. When the storm moves on, it leaves the streetlights blinking awake, yellow as apology notes. I carry the dog inside, barefoot, promising nothing, carrying the quiet like loose change I’ll forget to spend.

message.content:

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
